_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/Coarctation of Aorta/ : Coarctation of aorta_Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + Coarctation of aorta_PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: M.Ch. or DNB/ equivalent (cardiovascular thoracic surgery)
clinical_indications: Milder obstructions may benefit from intervention by decreasing left ventricular diastolic pressure and preserving left ventricular function in the long term.

Intervention especially in the presence of hypertension at rest, abnormal blood pressure response during exercise, progressive left ventricular hypertrophy, and in cases of complex heart disease particularly Fontan patients.
Peak-to-peak coarctation gradient ≥ 20 mg (difference in peak pressure proximal and beyond the narrowed segment).
Peak-to-peak coarctation gradient < 20 mg with imaging evidence of significant coarctation and radiologic evidence of significant collateral flow.

clinical_thresholds.note: difference in peak pressure proximal and beyond narrowed segment
with imaging evidence of significant coarctation and radiologic evidence of significant collateral flow

claim.label: Barcode of the balloon/implant, If used

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes show detailed history, signs & symptoms, and indication for procedure?
Echo/Doppler report and Stills are suggestive of Coarctation of Aorta?
Patient's Echo/angiogram stills report shows Coarctation of Aorta?

cpd_claim.q: Detailed Procedure/ Operation notes are submitted?
Post Procedure Echo/Angiogram stills with report show increase in diameter of the affected part of aorta?
Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up is submitted?
Invoice of balloon used is submitted?

common_queries: Pre-authorization Echo/angiogram stills report does not show Coarctation of Aorta.
Pre-authorization clinical notes are missing or lack detailed history, signs, symptoms, or indication for procedure.
Post-procedure Echo/Angiogram stills or report does not show an increase in diameter of the affected part of the aorta.
Invoice for the balloon used is not submitted at the claim stage.
Discharge summary is missing or does not include the date of follow-up.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Cardiac Catheterisation lab: Yes, CCU/ICCU: Yes, Qualified cardiologist (DM/ DNB cardiology): Yes, Qualified cardiothoracic surgeon (MCh/ DNB cardiovascular thoracic surgery): No
clinical_key_pointers: Coarctation of the aorta (C) is the sixth most common congenital lesion accounting for 4-6 per cent of live births with congenital heart disease.
Anatomical spectrum may vary from discrete narrowing of the thoracic aorta at the insertion of the ductus arteriosus to tubular hypoplasia.
Effect of narrowing includes increased afterload on the left ventricle, exposure of the upper body to hypertension, flow disturbance in the thoracic aorta, and decreased perfusion to the lower body.
Symptoms (babies with severe coarctation): Pale skin, Irritability, Heavy sweating, Difficulty breathing, Difficulty feeding.
Left untreated, aortic coarctation in babies might lead to heart failure or death.
Symptoms (older children and adults with less severe coarctation): Headache, Muscle weakness, Leg cramps or cold feet, Nosebleeds, Chest pain.

Signs: High blood pressure in the arms, A blood pressure difference between the arms and legs (higher in arms, lower in legs), A weak or delayed pulse in the legs, A heart murmur (an abnormal whooshing sound caused by faster blood flow through the narrowed area).
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Infants with "critical” coarctation: Identification essential to maintain patency of the ductus prior to surgical repair by continuous intravenous infusion of prostaglandin E1.
